prompt: Documentary photograph of a professional translator seated at a wide wooden desk in a dimly lit study. A long, dense foreign-language manuscript is spread across the entire desk. The majority of the pages are in deep shadow — peripheral, unattended. But three or four specific passages are brightly illuminated by a directed brass desk lamp, each surrounded by dense handwritten margin notes and marked with small translucent sticky tabs. The translator's gaze is locked on one illuminated passage, pen in hand mid-sentence. Their eyes are clearly returning to these same lit passages repeatedly — the handwriting around them is layered and dense, evidence of many visits. The unlit portions of the manuscript are ignored entirely. The contrast between the bright focal passages and the dark peripheral text is stark. Warm amber lamp light on the illuminated sections, cool shadow everywhere else. Documentary photography style. Photorealistic, natural depth of field. No diagrams, no graphs, no text legible, no labels, no mathematical notation, no equations, no floating annotations, no non-physical elements, no neural network visualisations, no data flow arrows, no UI elements, no screen displays, no infographic elements. Everything in the scene must be a physical object that could exist in the real world.
metaphor: Translator illuminates only key manuscript passages while the rest recedes into shadow. Differential focus on specific input positions encodes the weighting mechanism directly.
